fuzzy_prd:
We need a small utility library that lets our system persist and reload deployment configuration records in two standard text-based interchange formats. Each configuration record describes a single deployable component: a short identifier, the name of its backing implementation, and an ordered list of named startup settings. Groups of records can be packaged into a collection for bulk deployment operations.

The library must convert a single record or an entire collection into either format, and read those formats back into in-memory objects. When reading a collection document it should gracefully ignore extra descriptive fields not part of the core model — such as human-readable labels or localization hints — rather than failing.

After a parse operation, reported state must be fully deterministic: collection size first, then each record in document order, and within each record the startup settings listed with keys sorted alphabetically. Every output line must end with a newline.

The internal business logic must stay completely separate from the command-routing layer. The routing adapter reads a structured request from standard input and delegates to the core library. A specific internal component — findable by searching the portal package — acts as the central serialization helper wiring together both format encoders. A dedicated entry point class in that same package handles request dispatching. The full suite of format round-trip checks lives under a test script that accepts a directory of case files.

One small thing to make explicit on the JSON shape: for a single portlet object the key order is fixed as 'portlet-name' first, then 'portlet-class', then 'init-param'. For a collection JSON object there is a single key 'portlet' whose value is an array of portlet objects, and each of those portlet objects follows that same key order. So the collection form is exactly one object with exactly one key named 'portlet' (not 'portlets', not 'portlet-list'), with the array under it, like {"portlet":[{"portlet-name":"...","portlet-class":"...","init-param":[...]}]}.

Also worth calling out the difference between serialization order and parse dump order for init params, since that came up. During serialization (to_json, to_xml) init-param entries are emitted in the order they were supplied to the builder, so insertion order. During parse dump output (from_json, from_xml, from_xml_roundtrip) the init.<name>=<value> lines are sorted in ascending lexicographic order by parameter name, no matter what order they showed up in the incoming document.

For the dispatcher request, the JSON uses 'op' with one of to_json|to_xml|from_json|from_xml|from_xml_roundtrip, and 'target' with portlet|portlet_list. Then it takes either 'portlet' for a single object with portlet_id, portlet_class, init_params or 'portlets' for an array of those objects when it’s a serialization op, or 'payload' as a string for parse ops. Just to avoid confusion, the internal field names use underscores — portlet_id, portlet_class, init_params — while the wire names use hyphens — portlet-name, portlet-class, init-param.

On the XML side, a serialized portlet collection uses '<portlet-app>' as the root element with one '<portlet>' child per record. A single portlet serialization uses '<portlet>' as the root element. There is no additional wrapper around <init-param> elements inside <portlet>; they sit directly under <portlet> as siblings. Each initialization parameter in XML is an '<init-param>' element containing a '<name>' child element and a '<value>' child element. In JSON each init param is an object with keys 'name' and 'value', and the array field on the wire is 'init-param', not 'init_params'.

And for the implementation location the team asked about, this all lives under package com.dotmarketing.business.portal, containing DotPortlet, PortletList, InitParamTuple (as InitParam), SerializationHelper, XMLSerializable, XMLEnabledBuilder, and RcbDispatcher. The specific helper is SerializationHelper.java in com.dotmarketing.business.portal — holds static JsonMapper and XmlMapper instances, exposes toJson(), toXml(), fromJson(), fromXml() static methods.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception  [RcbDispatcher, package com.dotmarketing.business.portal]

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When parsing a collection from XML (<portlet-app>), unknown elements inside a <portlet> element (such as <display-name>, <resource-bundle>, or any other non-modeled element) must be silently ignored without error. The parser must not fail on unknown elements; only <portlet-name>, <portlet-class>, and <init-param> elements are extracted.

The deterministic text dump for a single parsed portlet has exactly four types of lines (no prefix): 'id=<portletId>
', 'class=<portletClass>
', 'init_count=<N>
', then one 'init.<name>=<value>
' line per parameter with names sorted ascending. No 'portlets=' header line is present for a single-portlet dump.

The deterministic text dump for a parsed portlet collection starts with 'portlets=<N>
', then for each portlet at index i (0-based, document order): '[i] id=<portletId>
', '[i] class=<portletClass>
', '[i] init_count=<N>
', then '[i] init.<name>=<value>
' for each param with names sorted ascending. All lines end with '
' including the last.

All serialization operations (to_json, to_xml) print the raw wire string followed by exactly one trailing newline character ('\n'). The wire string itself does not contain a newline; the newline is appended by the dispatcher after printing the result.

The 'from_xml_roundtrip' operation (target must be 'portlet_list') builds a PortletList from the input 'portlets' array, calls toXml() on that list (using PortletList's own XMLSerializable.toXml() entry point), then rebuilds a fresh PortletList using PortletList.builder().fromXml(xml). The rebuilt collection is reported using the same deterministic dump format as a regular from_xml collection parse.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: dedicated entry point class in that same package that handles request dispatching
context_pointers.actual_meaning: RcbDispatcher.java in com.dotmarketing.business.portal — reads JSON from stdin, routes by 'op' and 'target' fields, delegates to SerializationHelper and domain objects

context_pointers.vague_reference: a dedicated test script that accepts a directory of case files
context_pointers.actual_meaning: rcb_tests/test.sh — runs the full test suite, accepts --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases), writes output to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename.stem>@<case_index.zfill(3)>.txt

context_pointers.vague_reference: deployable component records grouped into a collection for bulk deployment operations
context_pointers.actual_meaning: DotPortlet (single portlet descriptor) and PortletList (immutable list of DotPortlet objects, serialized as JSON {"portlet":[...]} or XML <portlet-app>)

oracle_data.fallback_response: This library implements bidirectional JSON/XML serialization for portlet descriptor objects. Key classes are DotPortlet (single descriptor), PortletList (collection), SerializationHelper (format encoders/decoders), and RcbDispatcher (CLI adapter). Wire field names use hyphens (portlet-name, portlet-class, init-param) while internal/input fields use underscores (portlet_id, portlet_class, init_params). Parse dump output sorts init-param keys alphabetically.

feature1_1_record_to_json.description: Serialize a single portlet record into its canonical JSON wire form. A portlet record carries a portlet identifier, a fully-qualified portlet implementation class name, and an ordered set of named initialization parameters. The serializer emits a single JSON object whose keys are the hyphenated wire names: the identifier under 'portlet-name', the class under 'portlet-class', and the initialization parameters under 'init-param' as an array of objects, each holding a 'name' and a 'value'. Parameters are emitted in the order they were supplied. The raw JSON string is printed followed by a newline.

feature1_2_collection_to_json.description: Serialize a collection of portlet records into its canonical JSON wire form. The collection is rendered as a single JSON object with one key, 'portlet', whose value is an array of portlet objects in the order supplied. Each element uses the same hyphenated wire names as an individual record ('portlet-name', 'portlet-class', and an 'init-param' array of name/value objects). The raw JSON string is printed followed by a newline.

feature2_collection_from_json.description: Parse a JSON wire document back into a portlet collection and report the reconstructed domain state. The input payload is a JSON string in the canonical wire form (a 'portlet' array of objects keyed by 'portlet-name', 'portlet-class', and 'init-param'). The parser must rebuild each portlet record, exposing its identifier, its implementation class, and its initialization parameters as a name-to-value mapping. The reconstructed state is printed as a deterministic dump: the number of portlets, then for each portlet (in document order) its identifier, class, parameter count, and each parameter as a 'init.<name>=<value>' line with parameter names in ascending order.

cases.expected_output:
portlets=1
[0] id=categories
[0] class=com.liferay.portlet.StrutsPortlet
[0] init_count=2
[0] init.param2=param2.value
[0] init.view-action=/ext/c

feature3_1_record_to_xml.description: Serialize a single portlet record into its canonical XML wire form. The record is rendered as a '<portlet>' element containing a '<portlet-name>' element for the identifier, a '<portlet-class>' element for the implementation class, and one '<init-param>' element per initialization parameter, each wrapping a '<name>' and a '<value>' element. Initialization parameters appear in the order supplied and init-param elements are not wrapped in any container element. The raw XML string is printed followed by a newline.

feature3_2_collection_to_xml.description: Serialize a collection of portlet records into its canonical XML wire form. The collection is rendered as a single '<portlet-app>' root element containing one '<portlet>' child per record, in the order supplied. Each '<portlet>' child uses the same structure as an individual record ('<portlet-name>', '<portlet-class>', and unwrapped '<init-param>' elements). The raw XML string is printed followed by a newline.

feature4_1_record_from_xml.description: Parse a single portlet's XML wire document back into a portlet record and report the reconstructed domain state. The input payload is an XML string with a '<portlet>' root containing '<portlet-name>', '<portlet-class>', and one or more '<init-param>' elements. The parser must rebuild the record, exposing its identifier, its implementation class, and its initialization parameters as a name-to-value mapping. The reconstructed state is printed as a deterministic dump: the identifier, the class, the parameter count, then each parameter as a 'init.<name>=<value>' line with parameter names in ascending order.

feature4_2_collection_from_xml.description: Parse a portlet-collection XML wire document back into a portlet collection and report the reconstructed domain state. The input payload is an XML string with a '<portlet-app>' root containing one or more '<portlet>' elements. Each portlet may also carry descriptive elements that are not part of the portlet record model (such as a display name or a resource bundle); these unknown elements must be tolerated and ignored without error. The parser rebuilds every portlet, exposing its identifier, its implementation class, and its initialization parameters. The reconstructed state is printed as a deterministic dump: the number of portlets, then for each portlet (in document order) its identifier, class, parameter count, and each parameter as a 'init.<name>=<value>' line with parameter names in ascending order.

cases.expected_output:
portlets=3
[0] id=categories
[0] class=com.liferay.portlet.StrutsPortlet
[0] init_count=1
[0] init.view-action=/ext/categories/view_categories
[1] id=site-browser
[1] class=com.liferay.portlet.StrutsPortlet
[1] init_count=1
[1] init.view-action=/ext/browser/view_browser
[2] id=content
[2] class=com.liferay.portlet.StrutsPortlet
[2] init_count=1
[2] init.view-action=/ext/contentlet/view_contentlets

feature4_3_collection_roundtrip.description: Round-trip a portlet collection through its own XML representation: take an in-memory collection, render it to XML using the collection's own serialization entry point, then rebuild a fresh collection from that XML using the collection builder's parse entry point. This verifies the serialize/parse pair is mutually consistent (a value serialized and then parsed yields an equivalent value). The rebuilt collection's state is printed as a deterministic dump: the number of portlets, then for each portlet (in order) its identifier, class, parameter count, and each parameter as a 'init.<name>=<value>' line with parameter names in ascending order.

cases.expected_output:
portlets=1
[0] id=categories
[0] class=com.liferay.portlet.StrutsPortlet
[0] init_count=2
[0] init.param2=param2.value
[0] init.view-action=/ext/c
